content:
I am drawn to stories about the end of things.
These stories always have some definitive cataclysm, an event horizon to cross, beyond which the characters we love or our assumptions about the solidity of their world will never return — but this is never the most interesting part. I love tales about the end of the world because they present a dark puzzle, a mystery about how systems are built on the foundations of their own demise… and because they’re about rebirth, and transition, and because when you read between the lines they remind you that we’re all caught in an endless cycle of dying and being reborn in ways both big and small.
Stories about the end of the world remind me that I’m nothing to the universe, but that being nothing means that I’m also a part of everything. They make me feel small, and by feeling small, I begin to feel whole.
With accelerating intermit, I also write. I try to write about the things I love or things that make me feel afraid, so I’ve often tried to write about the end of the world, and about how that reminds me that I am a speck of profoundly consequential insignificance.
It’s no secret that I’ve oft fantasized about going Full Thoreau: to go to the woods, to live deliberately, to live deep and suck out all the marrow of life. Because… when I have most successfully connected with art and literature, and with nature, I am made to feel small. I begin to understand that the bedrock assumptions I have about existing are illusory, that beginnings and endings and the hard edge of ego are imagined things which allow me to believe that me is a thing that matters.
It’s a flight of fancy, though. Here in the Real World, there are bills to pay, TPS’s to report, sheets to spread. These words on this page will never feed my family, they’ll likely never reach beyond the handful of people I know. Living deeply for me requires that I remain connected to our modern systems, to the people I care about and who care about me, and so long as I am a part of their world, I will not go to the woods for more than a respite.
I accepted that Thoreau’s path is just a fantasy. I similarly accepted long ago that I’d never make a living as a writer. I’ve learned that I can write things that are real, and sometimes even things that are deep, but that I cannot easily write things that are accessible. I can’t produce a product that people will purchase.
It’s a problem of ego, I think. Fiction allows us to distance ourselves from our truths, to tell them with an outsider’s perspective … but I struggle with that distance. When I write, I’m either following a through line so far removed from my actual point that even I lose the thread, or I’m so literal that it really isn’t fiction anymore.
It doesn’t help that I’m in love with prose, either. I mean, look at this mess:
We’d gathered to bury my brother near the river’s soured shore. My hammer rang as I stamped his name into the monolith, his passing the latest in a lineage at least a thousand years long. Too many entries shone fresh against the weather-worn surface, glittering under the dull glow of our ruined star.
I let the hammer drop, and I stood, and I let my forehead rest against the cool steel. I wondered, as I always did on days like these, if the last of us would leave their own name here before their time came.
I wanted to write a story about ecological collapse; I wanted it to be set inside of some massive edifice constructed by long-forgotten fore-bearers, where people live their small lives, insignificant when compared to burden of their own profound history, but reminded constantly that the saga wouldn’t exist without their tiny lives.
I wrote that cold open and I fell in love with it and I stopped. And I remembered that maybe I’ve been teased by the muse from time to time, but, really, I’m just playing with language.
That’s okay! I’m sad about it sometimes, but I’ve accepted it. I’m good at other things — I’m pretty good at solving puzzles. I can consume disgusting volumes of detail and systematize them. I’ve occasionally been accused of being smart and kinda and hard working.
So I didn’t turn out to be a writer, but I discovered that a lot of the beauty we find in language also appears in computer programming, or really, in kind of system where humans are interfacing with machines. It’s not Thoreau, but there’s a sharp-edged elegance to well-formed software. I learned that I could still be a craftsman, and even occasionally an artisan, in a space that was ultimately ground between the competing interests of machine logic and the flaming merry-go-round of capitalism, and I could make a living there. It’s not Thoreau, but maybe it’s a little bit of Hunter S. Thompson, and honestly, I’m probably a little happier when I accept that I’m some kind of gonzo.
I’ve done this for a couple of decades now. “Computer touching.” It’s mostly just work, just a job like we’ve all got, a fiscal necessity full of made up goals and tasks and egos. Still, there have always been these little moments where I can walk that narrow path between the needs of “Engineering” and “Business” to tap my artistic impulses. I think both the engineering and business needs were better for it, even.
Lately though, we have machines that can do a big chunk of this work. Or that can, at least, stamp out variations on solved themes quickly enough that the business monster became overfed. Engorged, voracious, and impudent, it has trampled across my thin silver line of art to feed on all of my profession’s engineering. The business monster has been doing that for a long time of course, but always in furtive raids instead of this blitzkrieg of value extraction. There was, for a time, a kind of symbiotic balance.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Justin Ha, via Usplash: https://unsplash.com/@mekanizmSo here I am. I never was a writer, not in any sense that will matter to anyone I’ve never personally met, but that spark of poetic romanticism did manage to combine with some of my other traits, to grant me a profession that was fulfilling, often enough.
My profession is dying now, here in the middle of my life. Everything in software, in technology, everything everywhere has been poisoned by “deliverables,” and a soulless machine that can deliver (sort of) faster than I ever could. It doesn’t feel great, but I’m reminded that this has happened before. Artisans gave way to industrialists; horses gave way to cars, writing computer software is giving way to begging the computer to do things for you.
I’ve become obsolete before — setting aside the burden of soldiering, even after the brief time I carried it, felt like an end of one world, a beginning of another. But this is different somehow. I can’t shake the feeling that we’re ablating our humanity, our ability to engage with art, our understanding of what it means to map hard reality to our weird mammal brains. We’re flesh that somehow learned how to paint, to carve sculptures, to extract meaning from an indifferent universe. We somehow learned to be more than the sum of our animal parts, but also somehow nothing more than a bunch of silly apes scurrying around doing the same mammal things as our furrier cousins. Are we forgetting what we are, when the things we create don’t force us to confront that paradox of being both more and less than animals?
I look at people ‘creating art’ with generative AI, and being pleased with the output. I’m a little envious because it just doesn’t resonate for me. I feel like I’m missing out, I feel alienated, because I see these things, and I just feel empty. So many people I know are enthralled by pixel and syntax generators, but I’m just tired, and I’m reminded of why I engage with art: not because of the end product, but by wondering what the artist felt, what they intended to convey, which wounds they were overcoming to create this thing that’s reaching across time and space to tickle my fickle monkey brain.
I look at people creating software with the same tools, and I wonder what happens when we’ve sucked all of the art out of this endeavor. Does it matter? Did it ever matter? What happens when none of the tiny human triumphs and hardships that go into making something are there anymore?
I don’t know. I’m terrified, in fact, about how little I know about the things I’ve assumed to be stable truths in my world.
I wonder often about the human effort and ecological inputs that make up the food I eat or the cheap generic clothes I wear, but I think I’m largely alone in this. I’m ruminating constantly about the systems we’re a part of, about how little our attachment to art or whimsy matter to the whole, about how we’re each so profoundly insignificant to an indifferent world, and about how despite that insignificance, taking away any of these individually meaningless details would cause the entire structure to collapse. I think that a lot of us are content to drift along in a world that delivers our needs to us, minimizing our connection to the people and ecosystem that make these things as possible. I envy that too, that ability to see the world as a simple thing. I don’t know how to turn off the deluge of details I perceive, or the relentless need to see how they’re all a part of a system. I don’t know how to see little bits of art and soul get sucked out of a system without feeling like it’s a big problem, actually.
It feels to me like a little the apocalypse — it’s the end of one world, hopefully the beginning of another, but I don’t know how to handle it. I’ve read these stories about ramshackle cathedrals collapsing into themselves, and sometimes I think I’ve taken their lessons to heart. Sometimes I’ve understood that systems collapse and that new things rise from the ashes, but it’s so much easier to be hopeful when you aren’t living through it. I’m struggling with the uncertainty of not really knowing if we’ve passed the point of no return and with the frustration of not having an omniscient third person to show me all of the important details. I’m struggling with feeling obsolete and aimless and insignificant, but in a new way that doesn’t inspire the sort of awe I feel about being tiny in a vast universe.
I’ve followed so many fictional characters who’ve lived through the end of the world, and I’ve been awestruck by the vast scope of these stories, but the more I think about the Big Things, the more I realize that I’ve never really taken the time to understand that the world ends every day, in a million little ways, and that we don’t notice most of them.
Maybe the end of the world wasn’t ever such a big deal in the first place, no matter how despondent I feel about it today. Maybe everything will be fine, and maybe I’m overthinking it. Those of us who need art as a tool to understand the world will find a path forward, undoubtedly; we will invent new ways to inject sublime nonsense into our lives, to discover meaning or to embrace the absurd and accept that there is no meaning.
After all of the time I’ve spent with these stories, though, I wish I’d learned something more about what happens after the novel ends. I wish I’d discovered something more about how to live through an apocalypse wrought from stupidity and hubris, a cataclysm that barely even matters.
I wish I’d learned more about how to be hopeful when it’s my world that’s ending. I wish I could know if the last of us will stamp our names into a monolith that no one will ever see.
